elements.sent-2411-001: We report on numerical simulations of the two-dimensional spin-$1$ Blume-Capel ferromagnet embedded in a triangular lattice.
elements.sent-2411-002: Utilizing a range of Monte Carlo and finite-size scaling techniques, we explore several critical aspects along the crystal field--temperature ($\Delta, T$) transition line.
elements.sent-2411-003: Wang-Landau simulations measuring the joint density of states in combination with the method of field mixing allow us to probe the phase coexistence curve in high resolution, determining the tricritical point $(\Delta_{\rm t , T_{\rm t )$ with improved accuracy and verifying the tricritical exponents.}}
elements.sent-2411-004: Extensive multicanonical simulations identifying transition points across the phase diagram characterize the Ising universality class for $\Delta < \Delta_{\rm t}$ with precise determination of thermal and magnetic critical exponents expected in the second-order regime.
elements.sent-2411-005: On the other hand, for $\Delta > \Delta_{\rm t}$, a finite-size scaling analysis is dedicated to revealing the first-order signature in the surface tension that linearly increases upon lowering the temperature deeper into the first-order transition regime.
elements.sent-2411-006: Finally, a comprehensive picture of the phase diagram for the model is presented, collecting transition points obtained from the combined numerical approach in this study and previous estimates in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-007: The Blume-Capel model is defined by a spin-$1$ Ising Hamiltonian with a single-ion uniaxial crystal-field anisotropy~\cite{blume,capel .}
elements.sent-2411-008: The fact that it has been very widely studied in statistical and condensed-matter physics is explained not only by its relative simplicity and the fundamental theoretical interest arising from the richness of its phase diagram, but also by a number of different physical realizations of variants of the model, ranging from multi-component fluids to ternary alloys and $^{3}$He--$^{4}$He mixtures~\cite{lawrie .}
elements.sent-2411-009: In recent years, the Blume-Capel model was invoked in order to understand properties of ferrimagnets~\cite{selke-10 , wetting and interfacial adsorption~\cite{fytas13 , but also the scaling of the zeros of both the partition function~\cite{leila and the energy probability distribution~\cite{macedo24 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The zero-field model is described by the Hamiltonian \begin{equation}\label{eqHamiltonian} \mathcal{H} =-J\sum_{\langle xy\rangle}\sigma_{x}\sigma_{y}+\Delta\sum_{x}\sigma_{x}^{2} =E_{J}+\Delta E_{\Delta},\end{equation} where the spin variables $\sigma_{x}$ take on the values $-1, 0$, or $+1$, $\langle xy\rangle$ indicates summation over nearest neighbors, and $J>0$ is the ferromagnetic exchange interaction.
elements.sent-2411-011: The parameter $\Delta$ is known as the crystal-field coupling and it controls the density of vacancies ($\sigma_{x =0$).}
elements.sent-2411-012: We should note here that the decomposition on the right-hand side of Eq.~\eqref{eqHamiltonian into the bond-related and crystal-field-related energy contributions $E_J$ and $E_\Delta$, respectively, will turn out to be useful in the context of the numerical simulations discussed below.}
elements.sent-2411-013: For $\Delta\rightarrow -\infty$, vacancies are suppressed and the model becomes equivalent to the simple Ising ferromagnet.
elements.sent-2411-014: The phase boundary of the model in the crystal field--temperature ($\Delta, T$) plane separates the ferromagnetic from the paramagnetic phase.
elements.sent-2411-015: The ferromagnetic phase is characterized by an ordered alignment of $\pm 1$ spins.
elements.sent-2411-016: The paramagnetic phase, on the other hand, can be either a completely disordered arrangement at high temperature or a $\pm1$-spin gas in a $0$-spin dominated environment for low temperatures and high crystal fields.
elements.sent-2411-017: At high temperatures and low crystal fields, the ferromagnetic--paramagnetic transition is a continuous phase transition in the Ising universality class, whereas at low temperatures and high crystal fields, the transition is of first order~\cite{blume,capel .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The model is thus a paradigmatic example of a system with a tricritical point $(\Delta_{\rm t , T_{\rm t )$~\cite{lawrie , where the two segments of the phase boundary meet.}}}
elements.sent-2411-019: At zero temperature, it is clear that ferromagnetic order must prevail if its energy $zJ/2$ per spin (where $z$ is the coordination number) exceeds that of the penalty $\Delta$ for having all spins in the $\pm 1$ state.
elements.sent-2411-020: Hence the point $(\Delta_0 = zJ/2, T = 0)$ is on the phase boundary~\cite{capel .}
elements.sent-2411-021: On the other hand, for zero crystal fields, the transition temperature is not exactly known, but it has been well-studied for a number of lattice geometries.
elements.sent-2411-022: For a picture of the phase diagram based on the data of the current work but also on some previous computations from Ref.~\cite{fytas_BC , we refer the reader to Fig.~\ref{fig:phase-diagram , where we fix units by choosing $J = 1$ and $k_{\rm B = 1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Since its original formulation, the model~\eqref{eqHamiltonian has been studied in mean-field theory as well as in perturbative expansions and numerical simulations for a range of lattices, mainly in two and three dimensions, see, e.g., Refs.~\cite{fytas_BC,fytas2012,fytas2013,zierenberg2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Most work has been devoted to the two-dimensional model on the square lattice, employing a wide range of methods, including real-space renormalization~\cite{berker1976rg , Monte Carlo simulations, Monte Carlo renormalization-group calculations~\cite{landau1972,kaufman1981,selke1983,selke1984,landau1986,xavier1998,deng2005,silva2006,hurt2007,malakis1,malakis2,kwak2015 , $\epsilon$ expansions~\cite{stephen1973,chang1974,tuthill1975,wegner1975 , high- and low-temperature series expansions~\cite{fox1973,camp1975,burkhardt1976 , and transfer matrix calculations~\cite{beale1986,kim17,blote2019 .}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{phase-diagram.pdf} \caption{Phase diagram of the two-dimensional spin-$1$ triangular Blume-Capel model in the plane of crystal-field coupling $\Delta$ and temperature $T$. The first-order transition line and tricritical point are determined from the field-mixing analysis using the Wang-Landau (WL) joint density of states. The inset magnifies the phase boundary near the tricritical point in the plane of $\beta = 1/T$ and $\mu = \Delta / T$, estimating the field-mixing parameter $r$ by its slope. The critical points obtained from the multicanonical (MUCA) calculations in this work and the earlier Wang-Landau simulations~\cite{fytas_BC} are also displayed in the second-order area, while the first-order transition points from the multicanonical calculations are not shown because they are visually identical to the Wang-Landau results. The complete list of the transition points is given in Table~\ref{tab:delta_c}. } \label{fig:phase-diagram}
elements.sent-2411-025: In this paper we are interested in the critical properties of the Blume-Capel model embedded in the triangular lattice, where $z=6$ and $\Delta_{0 = 3$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: For this particular case, Mahan and Girvin~\cite{mahan were the first to apply position-space renormalization-group methods to solve the model.}
elements.sent-2411-027: These authors estimated the critical frontier with the location of the tricritical point $(\Delta_{\rm t , T_{\rm t ) = (2.686, 1.493)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Many years later, Du et al.,~\cite{du performed a further sophisticated analytical calculation using an expanded Bethe-Peierls approximation to find respectively the estimates $(\Delta_{\rm t , T_{\rm t )=(2.841, 1.403)$~\cite{comment1 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The refinement $(\Delta_{\rm t , T_{\rm t )=(2.925(8), 1.025(10))$ was proposed in Ref.~\cite{fytas_BC via Monte Carlo simulations and an approximate scheme that takes advantage of the scaling behavior of the specific heat.}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Here we utilize a combined and robust numerical protocol based on the Wang-Landau and multicanonical algorithms, and corroborated by the Metropolis scheme, that facilitates the elucidation of several critical aspects of the triangular Blume-Capel ferromagnet.
elements.sent-2411-031: We propose a high-accuracy determination/characterization of the tricritical point using the method of field mixing and we provide a set of phase-transition temperatures mainly in the first-order transition regime.
elements.sent-2411-032: We analyze the scaling behavior of several observables in this first-order transition regime, focusing on the energy probability density functions, through which the scaling behavior of the surface tension is scrutinized upon lowering the temperature.
elements.sent-2411-033: Finally, we verify the expected Ising universality along the second-order transition line by computing the thermal and magnetic critical exponents of the ferromagnetic-paramagnetic transition and showcasing the logarithmic scaling behavior of the specific heat.
elements.sent-2411-034: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: In Sec.~\ref{sec:tricriticality , we present our findings on the location of the tricritical point and the proposed tricritical exponents, utilizing a field-mixing analysis based on Wang-Landau calculations of the joint density of states.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Section~\ref{sec:transitions discusses the transition features and provides a detailed finite-size scaling analysis along both segments of the phase boundary, supported by dedicated multicanonical simulations.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Finally, Sec.~\ref{sec:discussion offers a critical evaluation of our main results alongside a comprehensive overview of the model's phase diagram -- with a focus on the first-order transition regime leading up to the tricritical point -- and outlines directions for future research.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In this section, we identify the tricritical point and validate several conjectured tricritical exponents through a field-mixing analysis.
elements.sent-2411-038: This analysis is based on the joint probability distribution of $E_J$ and $E_\Delta$, which is calculated using the Wang-Landau algorithm in conjunction with histogram reweighting and conventional Monte Carlo methods.
elements.sent-2411-039: Below, we outline the numerical details of computing the joint probability distribution and explain how it is utilized in the field-mixing method to delineate the phase boundary curve and characterize the tricritical behavior.
elements.sent-2411-040: We employed the standard Wang-Landau algorithm~\cite{WL1,WL2 in triangular lattices and periodic boundary conditions with a single walker on the space of $E_J$ and $E_\Delta$ to measure the joint density of states $\Gamma(E_J, E_\Delta)$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Hereafter, $N = L \times L$ denotes the total number of spins and $L$ the linear dimension of the triangular lattice.
elements.sent-2411-042: While the current simulations maintain the same numerical framework as the earlier calculations conducted on square lattices by one of the authors~\cite{kwak2015 , we have adjusted the stopping criterion for the modification factor $f$ and the histogram flatness to accommodate the slower convergence observed in triangular lattices.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The flatness criterion for the histogram to update $f$ was set as $95 \%$ for $L \le 14$, $90\%$ for $L = 15$, and $80\%$ for $L > 15$.
elements.sent-2411-044: The stopping criterion of $f$ was set as $10^{-8}$ for $L \le 14$ and $10^{-7}$ for $L > 14$.

elements.sent-2411-046: The structure of $\Gamma(E_J, E_\Delta)$ appearing in the positive $E_J$ area reflects the frustration associated with antiferromagnetic coupling.
elements.sent-2411-047: Although we focus on the ferromagnetic model, this structure affects the Wang-Landau process since it needs to explore the entire energy space, slowing down the sampling dynamics needed to achieve the flatness criterion.
elements.sent-2411-048: Here, Wang-Landau data of the joint density of states were obtained for linear sizes $L = \{8, 9, 10, 12, 14, 15, 16, 18, 20, 21, 22, 24\}$.

elements.sent-2411-051: In computational time cost, the largest system size $L = 24$ of our current simulations in the triangular model is comparable to the previous calculations conducted on the square-lattice model with $L = 48$~\cite{kwak2015 .}

elements.sent-2411-052: Once the joint density of states is obtained, one can generate the probability distribution of an energy-based quantity $X_{E_J, E_\Delta}$ at a given set of $(\Delta, T)$ without additional measurements simply by enumerating \begin{equation}\label{eq:P(X)} P(X) = \sum_{E_J, E_\Delta} P(E_J, E_\Delta;\beta, \mu) \delta \left(X - X_{E_J, E_\Delta} \right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eq:P(X)} P(X) = \sum_{E_J, E_\Delta} P(E_J, E_\Delta;\beta, \mu) \delta \left(X - X_{E_J, E_\Delta} \right).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-053: Note that $P(E_J, E_\Delta;\beta, \mu)$ at any $\beta \equiv 1/T$ and $\mu \equiv \Delta / T$ is directly given by the density of states $\Gamma$ via \begin{equation}\label{eq:P_all} P(E_J, E_\Delta; \beta, \mu) = \frac{1}{\mathcal{Z}} \Gamma(E_J, E_\Delta) e^{-\beta E_J -\mu E_\Delta}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-054: In evaluating the probability density $P(X)$, we approximate the discrete spectrum of the $\delta$-function by Gaussian peaks with small finite broadening.
elements.sent-2411-055: Despite the advantage provided by the Wang-Landau joint density of states, we should note that it is only available for relatively small system sizes.
elements.sent-2411-056: To solidify our estimate of the tricritical point identified by the Wang-Landau data, we additionally considered the histogram reweighting (HR) technique \cite{FS1,FS2 based on conventional Monte Carlo calculations of Metropolis type at a specific parameter for larger systems.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The histogram $H(E_J,E_\Delta;\beta_0,\mu_0)$ measured at fixed $\beta_0$ and $\mu_0$ can be reweighted to generate the probability distribution of $E_J$ and $E_\Delta$ at nearby $\beta$ and $\mu$ values via \begin{equation}\label{eq:reweight} P(E_J, E_\Delta; \beta, \mu) \propto H(E_J,E_\Delta;\beta_0,\mu_0) e^{-\beta^\prime E_J - \mu^\prime E_\Delta},\end{equation} where $\beta^\prime = \beta - \beta_0$ and $\mu^\prime = \mu - \mu_0$.

elements.sent-2411-216: In this paper, we presented a comprehensive numerical and finite-size scaling investigation of the spin-$1$ Blume-Capel ferromagnet on the triangular lattice.
elements.sent-2411-217: In the first part of our study, we mapped the detailed line of first-order phase transitions, filling in a previously underexplored region of the phase diagram at low temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-218: Using Wang-Landau simulations to measure the joint density of states, along with histogram reweighting based on the Metropolis protocol and field mixing, we achieved a high-accuracy determination of the first-order transition line -- a summary of transition points is provided in Table~\ref{tab:delta_c -- and of the tricritical point at $(\Delta_{\rm t , T_{\rm t ) = (2.9388(2), 0.9850(8))$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-219: Additionally, via our combined numerical scheme we confirmed the tricritical Ising exponents $y_{t = 9/5$, $y_{g = 4/5$, and $y_h = 77/40$, up to a very good numerical accuracy.}}
elements.sent-2411-220: In the second part, we conducted extensive multicanonical simulations to investigate the finite-size scaling properties of the model in both its first- and second-order transition regimes.
elements.sent-2411-221: Our analysis yielded high-accuracy estimates of transition and critical fields, which can also be found in Table~\ref{tab:delta_c .}
elements.sent-2411-222: These align well with those stemming from the two-parametric Wang-Landau simulations carried out in the first part of our study, as well as with previous results reported in the literature~\cite{fytas_BC .}
elements.sent-2411-223: In the first-order transition regime, we identified key characteristics indicative of a first-order transition by examining the probability density function of the crystal-field energy and computing the surface tension of the transition using a method proposed by Lee and Kosterlitz~\cite{lee90,lee91 .}
elements.sent-2411-224: Our data indicate a linear increase in surface tension as the temperature decreases within the first-order transition regime.
elements.sent-2411-225: This finding is consistent with earlier analysis of the square-lattice model~\cite{kim17 .}
elements.sent-2411-226: Subsequently, we confirmed that the triangular Blume-Capel model falls within the universality class of the two-dimensional Ising ferromagnet in the second-order transition regime.
elements.sent-2411-227: This is characterized by the critical exponents $\nu = 1$ and $\gamma/\nu = 7/4$ for the correlation length and magnetic susceptibility, respectively, along with a logarithmic scaling of the specific heat.
elements.sent-2411-228: Closing, we propose an open research challenge: exploring the phase diagram of the triangular Blume-Capel model using the transfer matrix method with large strip widths enhanced by advanced Monte Carlo techniques and dedicated simulation platforms.
elements.sent-2411-229: This approach would enable the direct estimation of the phase diagram across multiple directions.
elements.sent-2411-230: It would provide valuable insights into the first-order transition regime, leveraging the exact and deterministic nature of the method.
elements.sent-2411-231: Additionally, it would facilitate the identification of the tricritical point and allow for a thorough examination of the second-order regime using phenomenological finite-size scaling based on the correlation length.
elements.sent-2411-232: Such an endeavor would not only complement our current findings but also build upon the successful work of Jung and Kim, who applied this method to the square-lattice Blume-Capel model~\cite{kim17 .}
